title: From High School Coach to Tech CEO: How to Build a Company by Accident
guestBio: Alyssa Nolte is the CEO of TruVue and host of two podcasts: The Growth Signal and Taking Back Monday. She's an accidental entrepreneur who built her tech company from the ground up, starting as a volunteer dance coach and evolving into a systems-thinking leader who believes intuition and strategy can coexist in business.
excerpt: What if your biggest business breakthrough came not from a boardroom strategy session, but from solving real problems with heart and grit? Alyssa Nolte's journey from volunteer dance coach to tech CEO proves that the most unlikely paths often lead to the most authentic leadership.
metaDescription: Discover how Alyssa Nolte accidentally built TruVue, a tech company, while balancing motherhood and leadership. Learn to embrace systems thinking and intuition.
keyTakeaways: Leadership skills can be developed in unexpected places, including volunteer coaching roles.
The most successful businesses often solve problems you've personally experienced.
Systems thinking and intuition are not opposites but complementary leadership tools.
Invisible labor in motherhood translates directly to valuable leadership capabilities.
Starting before you feel ready is often the key to breakthrough success.
Humor and authenticity can be powerful tools for leading through uncertainty.
Building a company accidentally can lead to more authentic and sustainable growth.

pullQuotes.text: Invisible labor IS leadership and women need to own that truth
Sometimes the best businesses come from solving problems you never intended to solve
You don't have to choose between systems and intuition - they work better together

timestamps.label: From Dance Coach to Tech Founder
The Origin Story of Form Dash and TruVue
Navigating CEO Life as a Mom

content:
Sometimes the most extraordinary leadership stories begin in the most ordinary places. For Alyssa Nolte, CEO of TruVue and host of The Growth Signal and Taking Back Monday, that place was a high school gymnasium where she volunteered as a dance coach, armed with nothing but determination and a desire to solve problems.

What happened next is a masterclass in accidental entrepreneurship and a powerful reminder that leadership isn't about having all the answers from day one. It's about showing up, solving problems, and trusting yourself enough to build something meaningful along the way.

The Unexpected Foundation of Leadership

Alyssa's journey challenges everything we think we know about traditional career paths and leadership development. While many of us wait for permission or the perfect credentials, she was busy developing real leadership skills in what might seem like an unlikely training ground. Coaching teenagers requires patience, creativity, problem-solving, and the ability to inspire others to perform beyond what they thought possible. Sound familiar? These are the exact skills that make exceptional CEOs.

The truth is, leadership development doesn't happen exclusively in corporate boardrooms or MBA programs. It happens when you take responsibility for outcomes, when you figure out how to motivate others, and when you're forced to think on your feet while managing competing priorities. If you've ever organized a family vacation, managed a volunteer committee, or coached any kind of team, you've been developing leadership skills whether you recognized it or not.

Building Solutions from Personal Pain Points

The origin story of TruVue, Alyssa's tech company, perfectly illustrates why the best businesses often come from solving problems you've personally experienced. What started as Form Dash grew from a genuine need she encountered in her own work, not from market research or investor demands. This organic approach to business building creates something invaluable: authentic product-market fit from day one.

When you build from your own pain points, you understand your customer intimately because you are your customer. You know exactly what keeps them up at night, what solutions they've already tried, and what they're willing to pay for. This insider knowledge becomes your competitive advantage in ways that external market research simply cannot replicate.

The lesson here isn't that you should only build businesses around your personal problems, but rather that you should pay attention to the friction you experience in your daily work and life. Often, those moments of frustration are pointing toward real market opportunities that others are experiencing too.

The False Choice Between Systems and Intuition

One of the most powerful themes in Alyssa's story is her rejection of the false choice between systematic thinking and intuitive leadership. Too often, we're told we need to pick a lane: either you're data-driven and logical, or you're intuitive and feeling-based. Alyssa's success with TruVue demonstrates that the most effective leaders integrate both approaches seamlessly.

Systems thinking provides the framework and processes that allow businesses to scale and operate efficiently. It's what turns good ideas into repeatable, sustainable operations. But intuition provides the wisdom to know when to break from the system, when to pivot, and when to trust a gut feeling that the data doesn't yet support.

This integration becomes even more powerful for women leaders who have been conditioned to apologize for their intuitive insights. Instead of seeing intuition as less valuable than analytical thinking, Alyssa's approach shows us how to honor both as essential leadership tools. Your ability to read between the lines, sense team dynamics, and anticipate problems before they show up in the data isn't a weakness to overcome—it's a strength to leverage.

Redefining Invisible Labor as Leadership Currency

Perhaps one of the most important conversations in this episode centers on invisible labor and its connection to leadership capability. The mental load that many women carry—anticipating needs, coordinating logistics, managing relationships, and ensuring nothing falls through the cracks—represents sophisticated project management and leadership skills.

When you're juggling school pickup schedules, managing family calendars, anticipating what supplies need to be restocked, and maintaining relationships with teachers, coaches, and other parents, you're demonstrating executive function at a high level. You're doing strategic planning, resource allocation, stakeholder management, and crisis prevention. These aren't just survival skills—they're leadership competencies that transfer directly to business success.

The problem isn't that women lack leadership experience; it's that we've been conditioned not to recognize or value the leadership we've been practicing all along. Alyssa's story gives you permission to count this experience, to name it for what it is, and to leverage it as you build your own leadership narrative.

The Power of Starting Before You're Ready

Throughout our conversation, one theme emerges repeatedly: the magic that happens when you start before you feel fully prepared. Alyssa didn't wait until she had a perfect business plan or complete technical expertise. She started with a problem she wanted to solve and figured out the rest along the way.

This approach isn't reckless—it's realistic. The truth is, you'll never feel completely ready for the big leap, whether that's starting a business, applying for a stretch role, or launching a new initiative. Readiness isn't a feeling you achieve; it's a decision you make. The learning happens in the doing, not in the planning.

If you're waiting for confidence to show up before you take action, you have it backwards. Confidence is built through taking action despite uncertainty, not by waiting until uncertainty disappears. Alyssa's accidental path to building a tech company proves that competence develops through engagement, not preparation.

Her story serves as both inspiration and permission: inspiration to see leadership potential in your current circumstances, and permission to start building something meaningful before you have all the answers. The high school gymnasium where her leadership journey began wasn't glamorous, but it was real. And sometimes, real is exactly what the world needs.

quiz.title: What's Your Hidden Leadership Origin Story?
quiz.description: Discover how your unique experiences and instincts are shaping you into the leader you're meant to be. Just like Alyssa Nolte's journey from high school coach to tech CEO, your path might be more powerful than you realize.

questions.question: When you're facing a problem that needs solving, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Trust your gut feeling about what might work, even if you can't explain why
Research thoroughly and create a step-by-step plan before taking action
Talk it through with others to get different perspectives and build consensus
Jump in and start experimenting, knowing you'll course-correct as you go

questions.question: Think about a time when you took on a role you weren't technically qualified for. How did you approach it?

options.text: You focused on the problems you could solve and let your natural instincts guide you
You immediately started learning everything you could to build proper expertise
You leaned heavily on building relationships and learning from others in similar roles
You figured you'd learn by doing and weren't worried about making mistakes along the way

questions.question: When leading a team or group, what feels most natural to you?

options.text: Adapting your approach based on what each situation or person needs in the moment
Creating clear expectations and consistent processes everyone can follow
Making sure everyone feels heard and valued, even when decisions get tough
Inspiring others to take risks and try new approaches, even if the outcome is uncertain

questions.question: Your friends would say your biggest strength in challenging situations is:

options.text: Your ability to see creative solutions that others miss completely
Your calm, methodical approach that helps everyone stay grounded
Your talent for keeping morale up and helping people feel supported
Your willingness to take bold action when everyone else is paralyzed by uncertainty

questions.question: When you think about your most meaningful work or volunteer experiences, they usually involved:

options.text: Solving a problem you personally understood or had experienced yourself
Building something sustainable that would continue to benefit others long-term
Working closely with people and seeing them grow or succeed
Breaking new ground or being the first to try something different

questions.question: If you were to start something new tomorrow—a business, project, or initiative—you'd probably:

options.text: Start with a problem you're passionate about solving, then figure out the details
Want to have a solid plan and sufficient resources before you begin
Focus on who you could partner with or who might benefit from what you're creating
Just start and trust that momentum and learning will carry you forward

results.title: The Intuitive Problem-Solver
results.description: You have an incredible ability to see solutions where others see obstacles, often trusting your gut even when you can't fully explain why. Your leadership style is organic and adaptive, and you're not afraid to build the plane while flying it. You thrive when solving problems you've personally experienced, which makes your solutions deeply authentic and effective. This episode will resonate with your journey of discovering that your unconventional path is actually your greatest strength.

results.title: The Steady Systems Builder
results.description: You're the person others turn to when they need structure, reliability, and thoughtful planning. Your leadership emerges through creating order from chaos and helping others see the bigger picture through clear processes. You believe in doing things right the first time and building sustainable foundations that last. This episode will inspire you to see how your methodical approach can be just as revolutionary as any dramatic pivot, and how your behind-the-scenes work is true leadership.

results.title: The Authentic Connector
results.description: Your superpower lies in bringing people together and creating environments where everyone feels valued and heard. You lead through relationship-building and genuine care for others, often using humor and vulnerability to break down barriers. You instinctively know that real leadership is about serving others and lifting them up. This episode will validate your belief that authenticity and connection are not soft skills, but the hardest and most important leadership capabilities.

results.title: The Fearless Pioneer
results.description: You're energized by uncharted territory and have a natural tendency to leap before you look, trusting that you'll figure it out along the way. Your leadership style is bold and inspiring, and you're not afraid to challenge conventional wisdom or try something completely new. You believe that perfection is the enemy of progress and that done is better than perfect. This episode will fuel your confidence that starting before you feel ready is not reckless—it's exactly how breakthrough success happens.

transcript:
**The Manager® Podcast**

Aimee: Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manager podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women as powerful leadership. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, entrepreneur and mama of six. 

Today's guest is someone I met about a year ago on Aviva Women's Roundtable hosted by our mutual friend Madori Varity. Right away, we connected over our shared experience as women in tech, moms of young kids, and creators with the systems mindset. She even bought a copy of The Manager, and we're diving into the kind of real life leadership the book celebrates.

Alyssa Nolte is the CEO of True View, host of The Growth Signal and Taking Back Monday, and what I like to call an accidental entrepreneur. She went from coaching a high school dance team to launching multiple tech ventures all while staying grounded, curious, and really funny. If you've ever wondered if you're qualified enough to build something bold or doubted whether motherhood and leadership can truly coexist, this episode is for you.

Alyssa Nolte, thank you so much for being on the show. It's so happy to have you today. Thank you so much for coming on. It's nice to see you again.

Alyssa: Yes, it's so good to see you as well.

Aimee: Yeah, it's been a minute. It's been almost a year.

Alyssa: God, it's amazing how time goes because it just feels like yesterday that we met on that round table and a bunch of total strangers from across the world find things in common, right?

Aimee: I know. Isn't that wild? I know. That was a really cool thing that Madori did to have those round tables. That's actually how I found my publisher for my book.

Alyssa: Oh, that's wild. I found a couple podcast guests from that round table and then of course it's just smart women doing incredible things and that's another one of the reasons why I was so excited when you reached out to join you on the show today.

Aimee: Oh my gosh. Well, I'm so happy to have you. Thank you so much for joining me. So, you call yourself an accidental entrepreneur. Can you take us back to that first leap when you said yes to startup life when everyone else was saying no thank you?

Alyssa: I really had no intention. I thought for sure that I was going to get a normal corporate job. I was going to go work for some company with a brand name that you've all heard of and I was going to climb the corporate ladder in that traditional path. I had explored grad school, maybe getting my MBA right out of college.

And I always joke if I'm ever going to give a graduation speech, it's going to be something to the effect of why you should take an unpaid full-time job right out of college. It's probably not going to be very popular with the people in the audience, but that's what I did. I had real offers. I could have done that corporate path, but there was this startup that was pre-revenue, just getting started, didn't have a client to speak of, and there was just something about it that made me excited.

And the idea that I would get to do something that if I showed up to work, things would happen and if I didn't show up, nothing would happen. And that really excited me that my everyday would actually mean something and would matter.

Aimee: Yeah, you're so right. In a startup, you know, you push the needle. Every individual that's involved in a startup really gets to push the needle for the company where in a way when you're working with a larger corporation you don't get to see how what you do, how your work affects the overall organization as a whole as much. It's easier to draw that connection in a startup. Not to say that people who work in corporate roles don't move the needle, but it's hard to connect yourself and your work to the overall success of the company because there's just more factors at play, right?

Alyssa: And the excitement for me of figuring out, okay, we've never sold anything at the startup. Okay, how do we get our first deal? Okay, how do we present our value proposition? How do we negotiate on price? Okay, what do contracts look like? And now that we've caught the car, what do we actually do now that we've convinced someone to hire us? 

And all of that, I discovered I loved the thrill of the solve and figuring out what is the thing that's in front of us. How can we systematically break it down and punch through that barrier and then on to the next.

Aimee: Yeah, that is so cool. And once you get used to that feeling it's, you know, as my husband and I say you become unhirable because you're hooked on...

Alyssa: I don't want to say I'm unhirable but I'm 100% unhirable. I would, I'm a great contractor. I'm a great freelancer. I'm a great consultant. We got acquired in 2020 and I worked at that company for three years and it was just hard. I wasn't used to having to have someone else approve my decisions or report up to anyone. So, it definitely was a chafing experience for me and I discovered I needed to just be out on my own and with all the risk and excitement that that involved.

Aimee: I totally feel you. I come from a family of deal junkies. My mom was an entrepreneur as you probably, if you skim through my book you would know, she's very much influenced my life in a big way. Once you get hooked on the hunt and the deal and the feeling of entrepreneurship, it's hard to go back.

Alyssa: It really is. For sure.

Aimee: So, tell me, I'm obsessed with this Form Dash origin story. How did you go from a volunteer coaching gig to leading a tech solution?

Alyssa: So, I was volunteering with a local high school dance team. And anybody who is familiar with dance or gymnastics or cheer, any of those sports that have a lot of expenses attached to them, it's really expensive to be a part of that team. Most teams spend anywhere from $2,000 per kid on the low side to $5,000 per kid normally, just depending on how competitive that team is.

And so I remember hosting a fundraiser and we had gotten invited to perform at the halftime of a bowl game. So, we were fundraising for flights and travel and going down to do this halftime performance, which was really exciting, but it was really expensive. So, I remember on a Friday night, I had a banker's bag full of cash from this fundraiser. It was $14,000. And I remember thinking, "Okay, I can't deposit this at the school until Monday. I have to hold on to this $14,000 until further notice." 

And I had this anxiety of any one of these parents could accuse me of mishandling the money and that's how I end up on the news. And my goal in life is to only end up on the news for good things and never be on the news for bad things. That's all I want. And so I was like what if something happens to the money? What if my house burns down? All these things that were happening that had no realistic reality of happening. But my brain started cycling through that and I said, "There has to be a better way for me to not have to handle $14,000 in cash so that I can still continue working with this team that I love, but not have to deal with the fundraising side of it." So, I started noodling on that solution.

And then a couple of weeks later, we did a fundraiser where we sold these little ice cream bars and I had these beautifully printed pieces of paper in which I couldn't read anyone's handwriting. I had to go through them manually one by one. I sat for hours taking things off a piece of paper and putting it into a spreadsheet. And then I discovered, okay, there has to be a better way to do this.

So, Form Dash was never intended to be a business. It was something that I had set up for myself to improve my daily experience, to streamline my process and to create a paper trail so that I could account for every dollar that passed through our program. Over time, other dance coaches, cheer coaches, other people who had to raise money in my circle said, "Hey, what's this Form Dash thing that you're using?" And when I said, "It's nothing. It's just something I built for myself. Don't worry about it." The reaction from every single one of them was, "So, can I use it, too?" 

So, then I had to figure out how do I monetize this and how do I turn it into a real business? Because it was never intended to be, just I never intended to be an entrepreneur. I never intended to build Form Dash. And yet I'm finding success because I'm solving problems that I'm facing rather than just trying to invent a problem.

Aimee: That's it. And that's exactly how Mamalicious got started, too. I was pregnant and I started making this prenatal protein powder in my kitchen. And I was just making it for myself. My mom was born with spina bifida. So I was trying to prevent a birth defect in my baby. And one of my interns for my film company, his girlfriend was a nutritionist and she was working with a celebrity client who was also pregnant at the time who said, "Hey, I want her to make me some of that." 

And that was the beginning. I looked at my mom. I said, "You're not going to believe who just ordered me to make a special batch of what I was calling Mama's Milkshake at the time." And my mom goes, "Aimee, you have a product. We need to go find a manufacturer and we need to go find some capital for this company." And that's exactly what we did. And it was accidental, the accidental entrepreneur. The stuff you never intended to be for anyone else but yourself turns out to be some of your most successful ventures.

Alyssa: Absolutely. Because you're just being a problem solver, you know, and here you are now you're building True View and you're building again something new. What inspired this venture and what problem are you solving with it?

Aimee: That original startup that I joined out of college included two college professors. One of them I'm still working with. And I had made a joke that he was never allowed to retire until I could figure out a way to upload his brain to the cloud. And we worked really well together. Our business was created on the idea that you can understand your customers more deeply. You can understand their thoughts, motivations, and desires and how do you get them to go from, hey, I'm interested in this product, service, whatever, to I'm ready to buy as quickly as possible, right? 

Giving them the right information at the right time in the right sequence can unlock that process. And so, we've been doing that since 2013 when I joined the company. And it's an expensive process. It's really only afforded by the Salesforces of the world, the Ciscos, the JP Morgan Chases, those who have seemingly unlimited budget, right?

Aimee: Yeah.

Alyssa: And for me as a small business owner, I wanted to figure out how do we unlock access to the same insights and information and accelerators that these big brands have had access to for the last 10 years and make it so brands of any size, whether you are a brand new company or someone more established, as long as you're not Salesforce, you can still have Salesforce level insights. So that's what True View is. It is unlocking everything I've learned about how customers make decisions and putting it at your fingertips even if you don't have a Salesforce budget.

Aimee: That's awesome. So, it's data. It's a way to analyze data.

Alyssa: Yeah, it's brand new modern customer intelligence. Not AI because everybody's slapping AI on it, but the fundamentals that power those AI systems that we've all come to know and love. Same fundamental principles here.

Aimee: How cool. That's, I mean and that's you're really helping to solve a problem that a lot of people have. You know there's so many companies that aren't in the Fortune 500. Pretty much everybody else and you know they've got access and they can try and they've got the brand name behind them and they've got everything lined up for them when they're these big brand names. But if you are a small company just getting started or trying to scale and grow, you don't have the same ability to take a risk and get it wrong as some of these bigger brands do.

Alyssa: Yeah. Oh, absolutely. Yeah. You don't have the billion dollar cushion to make mistakes that the other companies have.

Aimee: Yeah. Absolutely. Yeah. When you're a little guy, you have to make precision helps, you know, precision decision making, precision communication. If you can bring that to your customer, then you can give them a real advantage.

Alyssa: Exactly. That's what we're trying to do. Democratize access to data insights and customer intelligence and strategic decision-making.

Aimee: That's fantastic. Good job. Way to problem solve another yet another big problem. Love it.

Alyssa: I appreciate that.

Aimee: Yeah. So, you were one of the first women in tech to grab a copy of The Manager. Were there any parts that resonated with you or that made you feel seen on your own journey?

Alyssa: You talk about a conversation that you have with your son about burgers and why the world doesn't need more square burgers. And that's always one of the things that I have struggled the most with being a woman in tech, a woman entrepreneur, someone who's trying to grow a business because I'll be honest, I love what I'm doing. I love going to work. I have the best time working on my stuff. And I found it really hard to balance that relationship of I'm a mother and I'm a business owner and how to still be a parent when I still love my business, too.

And being able to say out loud, I do love my business and I do love working and I want to do it sometimes. And so, I've had a couple of conversations with my kids because I want to spend time with them. I want to be a good mom, but I also want them to look at me and say, "My mom is so cool. She has businesses and she builds things and people believe in her and people respect her." 

And so, I'm trying to figure out that balance between how do we teach them that we're incredible and we're smart and we're talented while giving them what they need to be successful and well-rounded children as well. And so your story about having that conversation about, yes, it's more economically viable to cut the burgers square instead of round. Well, Wendy's did that how many years ago? Made millions of dollars, billions of dollars doing it. 

Now, we have this next conversation of what does it really do for people when it comes to our innovations? Are we doing them simply because we feel we need to do something or is there really something here that we are changing or driving forward, right?

Aimee: Yeah. Finding the mindfulness. It's I feel this next generation, if we can teach our children anything, it's mindful entrepreneurship. It's yeah, it's cool to make money. Everybody wants to be a billionaire, you know, but it's how can we teach our children how to solve problems that need to be solved in order to save humanity, our planet, you know, that could actually improve life for the people that will be living on this planet 100 years from now. 

It's how can we instill the values of mindfulness into the future of entrepreneurs, into humans? I'm really hoping to inspire a whole generation of young people who can really maximize their potential in a way that's not just about how much money you have in the bank, but about your human potential as how we can influence the happiness and the quality of life of all of the beings that we're going to share the planet with in the future. You know, there's this deeper thing now.

Alyssa: Well, my son is 10, and we were talking in the pre-con conversation and he's 10. So, he's just starting to get to that understanding that he wants to buy things and that takes money and that to get money we have to do some work and we have to do some things. And one of the things we struggle with him is he wants to monetize everything. So he wanted to set up a YouTube channel so that he could stream himself playing video games so that he could monetize video games. And he wanted me to record him playing baseball so he could post it on his YouTube channel. 

And it just became every part of his life was what he sees on YouTube, which is these people MrBeast and other YouTube streamers and he wants to be just them. And I had to explain to him that most people don't make their money that way. And if you're going to create videos, I am totally supportive of you. But let's make sure they're videos that we're proud of, that the production quality is good, that it's not just another brain rot thing, and understand who we're making these videos for. 

So maybe I'm a little crazy, but I've been taking him through the process of who is going to watch your videos. Is it kids your age who are looking for age appropriate content? Is it kids who want to learn how to do something? Is it just people who want background noise and want that background noise on your YouTube channel? And then teaching them how to record in OBS and how to edit his videos and how to add thumbnails and do SEO optimization because I'm if we're going to do this, which you know, it's a 10-year-old's little fantasy right now. But if we're going to do it, we should do it in a way that you learn some skills that are valuable to you beyond just making Fortnite YouTube videos.

Aimee: I love that, mom. Way to go. Yeah. And you're teaching him a real skill, too. I mean, the future is video. I mean, it really is in a way that when I went to film school, I never would have thought that the future was going to be this video-heavy. I really didn't think it would be. I never would have imagined that you'd be shooting a podcast and there would be so much software and so much technology around creating and capturing video content. As you know most creators are creating in video content and you're giving him great data because you're coming from your business mind of okay let's who's your demographic kid, right? Who are we targeting?

And I've also noticed he's just so comfortable on camera where I feel my generation, I'm a millennial, I think we're super awkward because we didn't grow up in a world with a camera shoved in our face and all of our things that we posted on MySpace were pictures and it's all gone now. So, you know, we don't have to look back and cringe at ourselves.

Alyssa: It's true.

Aimee: But he is so comfortable and not concerned. And my hope is that he goes into the next generation of the workforce with these skills because I think they're going to be more important and vital than ever before. Being able to connect in this virtual digital remote environment, whether you're recording a podcast, recording content for TikTok, or just having a Zoom meeting. This is the way the world communicates now.

Alyssa: Yes. Yeah, it really is. And it's just Good job, mama. Good job. Way to teach him some real life skills.

I feel we have to, it's our duty as modern mothers to help our children navigate the technology that's at their fingertips now. And it's one of the things that sometimes they're doing it at school, but sometimes they're not. And you know, fully embracing the technology as it's coming at them. And I think that that's one of the things that we can do as future moms is help them navigate, you know, navigating technology in the way that we, you know, embrace it, but then we also create boundaries around it.

Aimee: Yeah. You know, it's yeah, let's, the way you're teaching him. Yeah. It can't just be about the money, dude. It's got to be about more.

Alyssa: Well, and boundaries are a good point, right? Because if he had his way, he would be on some form of technology screen something 24/7. And so we've had to balance his interest in it, right? And what we know is good for him, which is being well-rounded and well-developed and not just good at screen and tech things, but good at sports and good at keeping, you know, thinking about his food and keeping up with his chores and trying to create someone who is going to function well and bring value to society when he and it's time for him to enter society and do something that matters and not just exist in society, but drive it forward.

Aimee: Yeah. Yeah. A thousand percent. Couldn't agree more. I feel I'm struggling and up against the same thing with my kids. And the book that I'm writing right now is actually more focused on the young adult and it's about maximizing your potential in this technological future that we're moving into. And it's really something that is inspired by my 10 to 16-year-old boys because I have a 10-year-old almost 11, a 13 and a 16. And it's okay, how am I going to help these kids learn? Because if you're not using the technology, the technology is using you. And if you don't understand when you sign up for a website, if it's free, you're the product there. And if you don't understand when I spend, you know, $5.34 on Robux, that that is not coming back to you. And all of those things that go into it and how to be safe on the internet, right?

Alyssa: Yes. And there's so much there. I think that that book is going to be very important, especially as parents who maybe remember what the world was like before the internet and before cell phones try to navigate raising kids who aren't fully sucked into the metaverse and are ready to function in society.

Aimee: Yeah. I mean, our kids also need to know how to do dishes and do laundry and keep their rooms picked up and how to feed themselves actual food. I feel there's such a push for them to eat ultra-processed food and just sit on a screen all day long and you're fighting this the machine that's teaching these really bad behaviors to our children. It's that we have to remind them that they still have to function IRL too.

Alyssa: But to your point teaching them how to use these technologies to their best advantage, right? So going back to my 10-year-old, he will take my phone and he's very proud of this. He will take my phone and he'll take a picture of our fridge on the inside and send it to ChatGPT and say, "What should we have for dinner?" And he likes to help me cook it. But we integrate that technology experience of having ChatGPT figure out what we should be eating based on the ingredients that we have and then him actually taking that out of the fridge and following along with the experiment that ChatGPT gave us in order to cook dinner. And he's found that really exciting.

And from my perspective, it's teaching him to use the technology in new and creative ways. He uses it to do his SEO content for his YouTube videos, right?
